---
title: "Topological Graph Simplification Solutions to the Street Intersection Miscount Problem"
author: "Geoff Boeing"
format:
    revealjs:
        theme: [default, ../assets/reveal.scss]
        logo: ../assets/icon.svg
        menu: false
        transition: fade
        navigation-mode: linear
        controls-layout: edges
        progress: true
        include-in-header:
            - ../assets/font.html
            - text: |
                <style>
                #title-slide .title {
                    font-size: 50px;
                }
                </style>
        title-slide-attributes:
            data-background-image: ../assets/full_logo.svg
            data-background-size: 20%
            data-background-position: 50% 90%
        max-scale: 10
---

Counting is hard

::: {.fragment}

> Most real-­ world objects belong to fuzzy categories, resulting in subjective decisions about what to include or exclude from counts.

:::


::: {.fragment}

> challenges from sampling biases to arbitrary filtering to simple counting errors

:::



---

> counting street intersections can be nuanced and challenging in practice

---

## 3 challenges

- nonplanarity
- intersection complexity
- curve digitization

---

> Can we
> algorithmically simplify a street network graph's topology to
> produce a more accurate and performant model?

## Three Causes of Intersection Miscounts

---

![](../figures/reading/Screenshot%202026-05-26%20at%2014.12.19.png)

## The Problem of Miscounts

---

> intersection density
> (i.e., count normalized by area) is the most common measure
> of compact and sustainable street network design in practice

---

> too often, intersections are miscounted

---

> To correctly measure [...] we need accurate models.

# Algorithms and Analysis

## Edge Simplification Algorithm

---

![](../figures/reading/Screenshot%202026-05-26%20at%2014.19.02.png)

---

![](../figures/reading/Screenshot%202026-05-26%20at%2014.19.37.png)

## Node Consolidation Algorithms

---

### Geometric Node Consolidation

---

![](../figures/reading/Screenshot%202026-05-26%20at%2014.22.30.png)

---

### Topological Node Consolidation


::: {.fragment}

> things that lie near each other
> in two dimensions may be topologically distant from each other
> when constrained to a spatial graph embedded in three dimensions.

:::


---

![](../figures/reading/Screenshot%202026-05-26%20at%2014.24.01.png)

# Assessment and Validation

---

> We use OSMnx to model the drivable street network
> within each of these 8910 boundaries, then perform edge simplification in standard strict mode, and then perform topological
> node consolidation

---

> we count the intersections (nodes with degree > 1) before and after graph simplification.

---

> manual qualitative case study process across a random sample of 32 world urban
> areas

---

> compare these urban areas' graphs, by hand, to
> the original OpenStreetMap raw data and satellite imagery to
> verify that all graph nodes correspond to true intersections or
> dead ends

---

160 million nodes and 320 million edges -> 37 million nodes and 53
million edges

---

> 77% of the original nodes were interstitial

---

> The manual validation reveals that the edge
> simplification algorithm performs with 100% accuracy.

---

> reduces the median urban area
> intersection count by a further 12%, from 978 to 857 intersections

> traditional methods would
> overestimate the median urban area intersection count by 14%.

---

> Among nodes that were consolidated (i.e., the “positives”), the
> validation reveals a 98% true positive rate and a 2% false positive
> rate.

# Questions

---

Do you think that the chosen terminology is precise?

---

Is the analysis robust enough?

---

What are the limitations of these algorithms?

---

What is the threshold where one intersection ends?

---

What do you think about the structure of the paper?
